subject: ap-government
title: Quantitative Analysis — Political Participation of 18–24 Year Olds, 2018 and 2020
stimulus: Source: CIRCLE/Tisch College, 2020. Bar graph showing Political Participation of 18–24 Year Olds in the 2018 and 2020 Election Cycles, comparing various forms of participation (voting, donating money to a campaign, contacting officials, attending a march or demonstration, etc.) between the midterm (2018) and presidential (2020) elections.

parts.prompt: Identify the form of political participation that saw the largest change between the 2018 and 2020 elections, as shown in the bar graph.
parts.scoring_notes: Only 'donated money to a campaign' earns the point.

scoring_points.description: Identify the form of political participation that saw the largest change between the 2018 and 2020 elections, as shown in the bar graph.

required_elements: 1 pt: Identifies donating money to a campaign as the form of political participation with the largest change between 2018 and 2020.
alternatives.correct_example: Donating money to a campaign saw the largest change between the 2018 and 2020 elections.

parts.prompt: Describe the difference in overall participation between the midterm and presidential elections, as shown in the bar graph.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept any accurate description of the overall pattern. The response must compare midterm to presidential election participation, not just describe one.

scoring_points.description: Describe the difference in overall participation between the midterm and presidential elections, as shown in the bar graph.

required_elements: 1 pt: Describes that there was more political participation in the presidential election (2020) than in the midterm election (2018). Accept any accurate description that captures the overall greater participation in the presidential year across most or all categories.
alternatives.correct_example: Overall, there was greater political participation in the 2020 presidential election than in the 2018 midterm election — nearly every category of participation shown in the graph increased from 2018 to 2020.

parts.prompt: Draw a conclusion about how linkage institutions might have contributed to the difference in political participation between the midterm and presidential elections, as shown in the bar graph.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must connect a linkage institution to the participation difference — not just describe the difference. Accept any plausible conclusion about how linkage institutions contributed.

scoring_points.description: Draw a conclusion about how linkage institutions might have contributed to the difference in political participation between the midterm and presidential elections, as shown in the bar graph.

required_elements: Participation might have been weaker during the midterm election because there was less media coverage than in the presidential election.
alternatives.correct_example: Participation might have been weaker during the midterm election because there was less media coverage than in the presidential election.

required_elements: Participation might have been greater during the presidential election because interest groups provided more opportunities for youth involvement than during the midterm election.
alternatives.correct_example: Participation might have been greater during the presidential election because interest groups provided more opportunities for youth involvement than during the midterm election.

required_elements: Participation might have been greater during the presidential election because political parties might have concentrated on getting out the youth vote more than during a midterm election. NOTE: Acceptable responses do not need to identify a specific type of linkage institution.
alternatives.correct_example: Participation might have been greater during the presidential election because political parties might have concentrated on getting out the youth vote more than during a midterm election. NOTE: Acceptable responses do not need to identify a specific type of linkage institution.

parts.prompt: Explain how the different levels of political participation, as shown in the bar graph, might demonstrate the concept of political efficacy.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must connect the participation data to the concept of political efficacy (belief that one's participation matters or makes a difference). Accept either internal or external efficacy as a lens.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the different levels of political participation, as shown in the bar graph, might demonstrate the concept of political efficacy.

required_elements: If people believe that voting makes a difference, then they would be more likely to try to convince other people to vote.
alternatives.correct_example: If people believe that voting makes a difference, then they would be more likely to try to convince other people to vote.

required_elements: If people believe that midterm elections matter less than presidential elections, then they would be less likely to participate during a midterm election.
alternatives.correct_example: If people believe that midterm elections matter less than presidential elections, then they would be less likely to participate during a midterm election.

required_elements: If people believe that their government responds to the voices of the people, then they would be more likely to attend a march or demonstration than people who believe their government is not responsive.
alternatives.correct_example: If people believe that their government responds to the voices of the people, then they would be more likely to attend a march or demonstration than people who believe their government is not responsive.
